q:

Wolves were reintroduced into Yellowstone National Park in 1995. In particular, they were interested in changes to the population of elk, the wolves’ primary prey. The graph below shows the population size of the wolf and elk populations over time. The elk population steadily decreased in numbers.
The elk population remained stable, experiencing little changes.
The elk population reached carrying capacity and experienced overshoot.

The right vertical axis is labeled Yellowstone Wolf Population, and the numbers 0 through 200, in increments of 20, are indicated. The elk population in the period from 1996 to 2010 fluctuates between a high of approximately 14,000 in 1998 and a low of approximately 4,000 in 2010. q:
Tree die-off article - identify author claim
California’s Drought Killed Almost 150 Million Trees "The epic drought that began in the state of California in December 2011 and only officially ended in March of [2019] will have many lasting impacts—but one of the most severe is what it did to the trees in the central and southern Sierra Nevada mountains. A new study...reports that by the drought’s peak, a combination of factors...led to mass die-offs [totaling] almost 150 million trees.... [R]esearchers analyzed various forest communities...including areas where 80 percent of the trees died and other [sites] where as few as 2 percent of the trees were lost. They found that a number of factors came together to deal a devastating blow in the worst-hit areas of the forests.... [T]he roots of Sierra Nevada forests stretch down 15 to 50 feet, meaning that even during droughts trees can access moisture locked deep underground.... ‘Parts of the Sierra Nevada reached a “tipping point”...where annual precipitation plus stored subsurface water were not enough to meet the water demand of the forest,’ co-author Roger Bales of the University of California, Merced says.... ‘This forest die-off can be viewed as a “perfect storm”—the intersection of four years of low precipitation, hotter temperatures than in past droughts, and a heavily overstocked forest from centuries of fire suppression.’ Jacob Margolis at LAist reports that a similar drought between 1987 and 1992 was as dry as the recent drought period... [However, this] time around, temperatures averaged 2.16 degrees higher.... That led to a cascade effect; the heat caused accelerated evapotranspiration, in which water evaporates from leaves, leading to increased water consumption by the trees. Once the trees used up all available moisture and the roots began to dry out, the green canopies above also began to shrink. The stress also made the trees more susceptible to pathogens and insects, like bark beetles. All of that added up to major tree mortality.... Fifty-five percent more trees died off than the typical annual average—with nearly 80 percent of trees above 3,800 ft of elevation dying.... [R]esearchers say to expect more tree die-offs in the future as the planet warms. ‘We expect climate change to further amplify evapotranspiration and ground moisture overdraft...during drought,’ co-author Michael Goulden of the University of California, Irvine says.... ‘This effect could result in a 15 percent to 20 percent increase in tree death for each additional degree of warming.... We need to manage the forest like we would agriculture or other systems that are water limited, and not have as many trees there,’ he suggests.... [T]here is a small silver lining to the mass forest die-offs.... It gives land managers the chance to start with a clean slate and manage forests so they are more diverse, less dense and more resilient....” Copyright 2019 Smithsonian Institution. Reprinted with permission from Smithsonian Enterprises. All rights reserved. Reproduction in any medium is strictly prohibited without permission from Smithsonian Institution. Which of the following is the author’s primary claim in the article?
o: The management of forested areas using fire suppression techniques will help to decrease the frequency of wildfires and increase the health of California forests.

Frequent drought conditions combined with higher-than-average temperatures are increasing evapotranspiration rates in California forests, leading to stressed trees that are more susceptible to pests and disease.
Decreasing temperatures in California are causing more frequent periods of drought and a decline in a few tree species that have narrow tolerance ranges.
The overuse of forests for international exports is significantly reducing the size of forests in California and causing a decline in biodiversity.
